posts.content:
9 Excel mistakes that make you look like a beginner.
Most people make at least 4 of these without realizing it.
Swipe through and drop which one you are fixing first 👇
posts.first_comment:
Full breakdown of the Tables habit (Mistake #4) and why it is the single highest-leverage Excel change most analysts never make — in this week's Excel Flow with Mike Girvin.
https://rileystream.substack.com/p/the-four-languages-of-modern-excel

carousel_spec.title: 9 Excel Mistakes That Make You Look Like a Beginner (And How to Fix All of Them)

slides.title: 9 Excel Mistakes That Make You Look Like a Beginner
slides.subtext: Fix #4 and people will actually stop asking you for help

slides.title: Using VLOOKUP in 2026
slides.body: VLOOKUP breaks when you insert a column. Can't search left. Slows on large data. XLOOKUP does everything VLOOKUP does and more. If you're still teaching VLOOKUP, you're setting people up to unlearn.

slides.title: Merged Cells Everywhere
slides.body: They look clean. They break every formula that touches them. Use 'Center Across Selection' instead. Same look. Zero headaches. Your SORT and FILTER will work again.

slides.title: Hardcoding Numbers Inside Formulas
slides.body: =A2*0.08 means nothing to the next person. Put 0.08 in a labeled cell called TaxRate. Now =A2*TaxRate reads like English. The #1 cause of Excel errors in two major academic studies.

slides.title: Not Using Tables (the actual Excel Tables feature)
slides.body: Most people use Excel as a grid. Tables turn your range into a structured object. Formulas auto-expand. Filters built in. References become readable. The single habit that separates spreadsheet maintainers from spreadsheet fighters.

slides.title: Whole-Column References Everywhere
slides.body: =SUM(A:A) looks clean but forces Excel to evaluate a million cells. Use defined ranges or Tables. Your file performance will thank you.

slides.title: No Backup Before Editing Someone Else's File
slides.body: Ctrl+Shift+S → save a copy with today's date BEFORE you touch anything. You will mess something up. Past-you should give future-you the gift of an undo button.

slides.title: Color-Coding With No Legend
slides.body: Red, yellow, green cells — and no key explaining what they mean. The person who built it knows. Nobody else does. Either add a legend or stop the coding.

slides.title: Volatile Functions Everywhere
slides.body: NOW(), TODAY(), OFFSET(), INDIRECT() recalculate on EVERY keystroke in your workbook. One of these in 10,000 cells = an unusable file. Use them sparingly.

slides.title: Sheet1, Sheet2, Sheet3
slides.body: Name your sheets. INPUTS, CALCULATIONS, OUTPUTS, DASHBOARD. Anyone opening your file should understand its structure in 5 seconds. This is free.
slides.close_text: Which one are you fixing first? Drop it below. And follow for more Excel content every week.

Most people know 3 Excel formulas.
Power users live inside these 7.
Swipe through and drop which one changed how you work 👇

Full breakdown of when to use formulas vs Power Query vs DAX vs PivotTables — the four languages of modern Excel — in this week's edition with Mike Girvin.

carousel_spec.title: The Only 7 Excel Formulas You Actually Need for 90% of Real Work

slides.title: 7 Excel Formulas That Handle 90% of Real Work
slides.subtext: Most people use 3. Power users live inside 7.

slides.title: Find anything in any direction
slides.body: Replaces VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP. Searches any direction. Handles errors automatically. Learn this one first.

slides.title: Sum with multiple conditions in one step
slides.body: Stop building helper columns to filter before summing. SUMIFS does it inline. Same pattern for COUNTIFS, AVERAGEIFS, MINIFS, MAXIFS.

slides.title: Make your files look professional instantly
slides.body: Wrap any formula in IFERROR. Errors become clean readable text. One habit that makes every file you build look intentional.

slides.title: Format any value into any string
slides.body: Combine with & to build dynamic labels that update themselves every month, every quarter, every year.

slides.title: Filter without filters
slides.body: Returns a live, filtered list. Updates instantly when source data changes. The biggest single upgrade to Excel formulas in 20 years.

slides.title: The flexible lookup pair (still useful in 2026)
slides.body: XLOOKUP replaces this for new work — but you'll inherit thousands of legacy INDEX/MATCH formulas. Know how it works.

slides.title: Name your variables inside a formula
slides.code: =LET(rate, 0.08, qty, A2, price, B2, qty * price * (1+rate))
slides.body: Long formulas become readable. Calculations get reused without repeating. The modern way to write complex formulas.

slides.title: Which of these do you use every day?
slides.subtext: Drop your answer. And share the formula not on this list that you think belongs here.

posts.content:
How to build an Excel dashboard in under an hour.
No VBA. No prior experience. Step by step system that actually works.
Swipe through and save this for the next time someone asks you for a dashboard 👇
posts.first_comment:
Full dashboard build with the exact PivotTable, slicer, and chart setup — plus the KPI selection framework — in Excel Flow Edition #30.
[Substack URL for Edition #30]
Affiliate note: Maven Analytics is referenced. Link in the edition.

carousel_spec.title: How to Build an Excel Dashboard in Under an Hour (Step by Step)

slides.title: Build an Excel Dashboard in Under 1 Hour
slides.subtext: Step by step. No VBA. No prior experience required.

slides.body: Every good dashboard starts with one question: What decision does this help someone make? If you can't answer that in one sentence, you're not ready to build yet. Write the question first. The dashboard is just the answer.

slides.header: STEP 1 — Structure Your Raw Data as a Table
slides.body: Click anywhere in your data. Press Ctrl+T. Name it something readable like SalesData or OrderLog. Every formula you write now auto-expands when new data comes in.

slides.header: STEP 2 — Separate Sheets for Data, Pivots, Dashboard
slides.body: Never build dashboard + pivots on the same sheet. Create three sheets: DATA / PIVOTS / DASHBOARD. When something breaks (and it will), you know where to look.

slides.header: STEP 3 — Decide Your KPIs Before You Touch Design
slides.body: Pick your 3-5 KPIs. Total revenue. Units sold. Top category. Whatever the dashboard is for. Build those pivot tables FIRST. Design SECOND. Most people do this backwards.

slides.header: STEP 4 — Add Slicers for One-Click Filtering
slides.body: Click your pivot → Insert → Slicer. Choose your filter fields. Now your dashboard filters with one click. No dropdowns. No formulas. The single feature that makes non-Excel people feel like they can use your file.

slides.header: STEP 5 — Charts: Bar for Comparison, Line for Trends. That's It.
slides.body: No 3D charts. No pie charts with 12 slices. No donuts. Bar for categorical comparison. Line for time series. Two chart types handle 95% of dashboards. Resist everything else.

slides.header: STEP 6 — Layout: One Metric Per Section. White Space Is Not Wasted Space.
slides.body: Don't cram. Group related metrics. Use Excel's gridlines off (View → uncheck Gridlines). Add a title at the top. Looks finished, takes 90 seconds.
Review → Protect Sheet. Users can refresh and click slicers; they can't accidentally break formulas. The dashboard you ship in 60 minutes survives the next 60 weeks.

1. Too many metrics — if everything is important, nothing is.
2. No data validation — garbage in, garbage out.
3. Building for yourself, not the person who'll use it.

slides.title: What's the first dashboard you're building with this?
slides.subtext: Drop it in the comments. Save this for the next time someone asks. And follow for more step-by-step Excel content.
slides.footer_note: Want structured learning to go deeper? I recommend Maven Analytics' Excel + Power BI tracks. Affiliate link available in my Substack.

B) I forget it exists about half the time

Drop your most painful manual data cleanup that Flash Fill could probably solve in seconds.

By end of this year are you more focused on:

A) Knowing more — deeper skills, more functions, more power
B) Doing more — shipping better work faster with what you already know

Drop your answer. Then drop the one Excel commitment you are making this week. Monday starts in a few hours and this group is watching.

What is the Excel skill that took you the longest to feel actually confident with?

A) Power Query and M code
B) Dynamic arrays and spill behavior
C) DAX and Power Pivot
D) Building dashboards people actually use

Drop your answer and tell me what finally made it click.

Your XLOOKUP is returning the wrong value and you cannot figure out why.

Nine times out of ten the problem is not the formula. It is the data.

Check these in order:

Leading or trailing spaces in your lookup column. Fix with =TRIM(A2)
Numbers stored as text. Fix by selecting the column → Data → Text to Columns → Finish
Case sensitivity issues. XLOOKUP is case-insensitive but your source data might have inconsistencies that break the match logic

The fix that catches all three at once: wrap your lookup value and your search array both in TRIM and then in VALUE if you are matching numbers.
=XLOOKUP(TRIM(A2), TRIM(lookup_range), return_range, "Not Found")

This one formula change has fixed more broken lookups than any other single adjustment.

Drop the lookup error that has been driving you crazy. I will diagnose it in the comments.
→ Full breakdown of XLOOKUP edge cases in Excel Flow Edition #28: https://rileystream.substack.com/p/the-four-languages-of-modern-excel

Real scenario from an analyst I spoke with this week:
"I have a sales table with 50,000 rows. I need to sum revenue by region AND by month AND exclude returns. My SUMIFS formula works but takes 45 seconds to calculate every time I change anything."

Here is exactly what is causing it and how to fix it:

The problem is almost always one of three things. Either the sum range and criteria ranges are full column references instead of table references, or there are volatile functions elsewhere in the workbook forcing everything to recalculate, or the file has not been saved as .xlsx and is running in compatibility mode.

The fix in order:

1. Convert your data to a Table (Ctrl+T) and rewrite the SUMIFS using structured references
2. Set calculation to Manual (Formulas → Calculation Options → Manual) while you work, F9 to recalculate when ready
3. Save as .xlsx if it is not already

The structured reference version of that formula goes from taking 45 seconds to near-instant on most machines.

What is the slowest calculation you have ever had to sit through in Excel? Drop it below.

You are building a model that five other people will use. What do you protect first?

A) Lock the formula cells so nobody can break them
B) Add data validation so inputs stay clean
C) Color code inputs vs calculations so it is obvious
D) Write documentation on a separate tab nobody will read

Vote and tell me what you actually do versus what you know you should do.

You are building a report that updates monthly. You are manually changing the month name in the title every single time.

Stop.

Put this in your title cell:
="Monthly Report — "&TEXT(TODAY(),"MMMM YYYY")

Now your report title updates itself every month automatically. Combine it with your company name:
="Excel Flow Revenue Report — "&TEXT(TODAY(),"MMMM YYYY")

Take it further with EOMONTH to reference the previous month instead of the current one:
="Report as of "&TEXT(EOMONTH(TODAY(),-1),"MMMM YYYY")

One formula. Zero manual updates. Looks intentional every single time.

Drop the thing you are still updating manually every month that a formula could handle.
→ More dynamic date formulas in Excel Flow Edition #27: [Substack URL for Edition #27]

Real scenario:
"I have a workbook with 12 monthly sheets all with the same structure. I need a summary sheet that pulls the total from cell B47 on every sheet. I am currently writing the formula 12 times."

Here is the one formula that replaces all 12:
=SUM(January:December!B47)

That is a 3D reference. It sums cell B47 across every sheet between January and December in one formula. If your sheets are named differently just change the names.

The catch: 3D references do not work inside SUMIF, VLOOKUP, or most other functions. They only work with SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, MIN, MAX and a few others.

For everything else you need either Power Query to consolidate the sheets first or a helper column approach.

Drop your multi-sheet consolidation nightmare below. I will tell you whether 3D references can solve it or whether you need Power Query.

Power Query — where are you honestly?

A) Use it daily. It changed my life.
B) Know how it works but rarely use it
C) Have tried it but got confused and went back to manual
D) Have never opened it and I am not sure where it even is

Drop your answer and tell me the data cleaning task that finally pushed you to learn it or the one that made you give up.

You got a CSV export with dates that Excel refuses to recognize as dates. They look like dates. They are not dates. Nothing works.

This happens because the dates are stored as text in a format Excel does not automatically parse. Here is the fastest fix:

If your dates look like 2026-05-28:
Select the column → Data → Text to Columns → Delimited → Next → Next → choose Date → select YMD → Finish

If your dates look like 28/05/2026 (day first):
Same process but choose DMY instead of YMD

If neither works add a helper column with:
=DATEVALUE(TEXT(A2,"YYYY-MM-DD"))
Then copy → paste special → values only → delete the original column.

This fixes 95% of broken date imports in under two minutes.

Drop your worst date formatting horror story below.
→ Full Power Query data cleaning walkthrough in Excel Flow Edition #25: https://rileystream.substack.com/p/mynda-treacy-the-1-excel-mistake

Real scenario:
"I need to highlight every row in my table where the deadline has passed AND the status is not marked complete. Conditional formatting is only highlighting some of the rows not all of them."

The fix:
Your conditional formatting rule is probably applied to only the first column instead of the entire row. Here is the exact setup:

1. Select your entire table range first — for example A2:F100
2. Go to Conditional Formatting → New Rule → Use a formula
3. Enter this formula:
=AND($E2<TODAY(),$F2<>"Complete")

The dollar sign before the column letter ($E and $F) locks the column but lets the row shift. No dollar sign before the row number (2) lets the formatting check each row independently.

This is the single most common conditional formatting mistake and it causes exactly the symptom you described.

Drop your conditional formatting question below. If it is broken I will rewrite the rule for you.

Honest question. What does your average Excel workbook look like when you first open it in the morning?

A) Organized, documented, exactly where I left it
B) Functional but I am the only one who understands it
C) A collection of decisions I made under deadline pressure
D) I try not to think about it

Drop your answer and tell me the one thing you would go back and fix if you had the time.

You are using COUNTIF to count unique values. It is giving you the wrong number.

COUNTIF counts occurrences not unique values. Here is the formula that actually counts uniques:
=SUMPRODUCT(1/COUNTIF(A2:A100,A2:A100))

How it works: COUNTIF returns how many times each value appears. Dividing 1 by that number gives you a fraction. SUMPRODUCT adds all the fractions. The math works out so that duplicates cancel and you get the true unique count.

The limitation: this breaks if there are blank cells. Add an IF to handle blanks:
=SUMPRODUCT((A2:A100<>"")/COUNTIF(A2:A100,A2:A100&""))

If you are on a version of Excel with dynamic arrays use this instead — it is cleaner:
=COUNTA(UNIQUE(A2:A100))

Drop the counting problem you have been solving with a workaround.
→ Dynamic array functions deep dive in Excel Flow Edition #27: [Substack URL for Edition #27]

Real scenario:
"My manager asked me to build a report that automatically emails itself every Monday morning. I know Excel can do this but I have no idea where to start."

Here is the honest answer:
Excel alone cannot send emails. You need one of these three approaches depending on what your company uses:

Option 1 — Power Automate (if your company uses Microsoft 365). Build a scheduled flow that opens your Excel file, refreshes the data, and sends it as an attachment. No code required. Takes about 20 minutes to set up.

Option 2 — VBA + Outlook. If you have Outlook and want everything inside Excel, you can write a VBA macro that sends the file. Requires Outlook to be open on your machine when it runs.

Option 3 — Python script scheduled with Windows Task Scheduler. Most powerful and most portable. Takes longer to set up but runs without any Microsoft apps being open.

For most analysts at a company using Microsoft 365, Option 1 is the answer. It is free, requires no IT approval in most organizations, and takes less than half an hour.

Drop the Excel automation task you have been meaning to set up. I will tell you which approach fits your situation.

End of week. What Excel thing are you leaving unfinished on your desk today?

A) A dashboard I started but could not get clean in time
B) A formula I could not get to work and ran out of patience
C) A file I inherited and still do not fully understand
D) Something I should have automated months ago

Drop it below. Let this community help you finish it or at least make peace with it.

You are building a dashboard and your pivot table numbers do not match your raw data totals. This is one of the most stressful things that happens in Excel and it almost always has one of four causes.

Check these in order:

1. Your pivot table data source range does not include all the rows. Right click the pivot → Change Data Source → make sure the range covers everything including new rows added since you built it.
2. You have filters applied to your source data. Pivot tables include filtered-out rows in their totals. Clear all filters from the source before refreshing.
3. There are duplicates in a column the pivot is summing by. It is summing correctly but you are comparing it to a number that was manually de-duplicated.
4. The date field is grouping by month when you expect it to be grouping by individual date. Right click the date column in the pivot → Ungroup.

Work through these four in five minutes and 90% of pivot discrepancies resolve themselves.

Drop your worst "the numbers do not match" story below.
→ Full dashboard build walkthrough in Excel Flow Edition #30: [Substack URL for Edition #30]

Real scenario:
"I need to pull data from 15 different Excel files all sitting in the same folder. They all have the same structure. Right now I copy and paste from each one manually every week."

This is exactly what Power Query was built for.

Here is the approach in four steps:

1. Data → Get Data → From File → From Folder
2. Select the folder containing all 15 files
3. Power Query loads a preview of every file. Click Combine → Combine and Transform
4. Power Query opens one file as a sample. Clean it once — remove columns, rename headers, set data types. Click Close and Load.

Now every week you just click Refresh and Power Query pulls all 15 files, applies your cleaning steps, and loads the combined data in seconds.

The one requirement: every file needs the same structure. If the headers or column order differ between files you need to add a normalization step in Power Query first.

Drop the multi-file consolidation task you are still doing manually. I will walk you through whether this approach solves it.

What is the Excel thing you are working on this weekend that has nothing to do with your job?

A) A personal budget or financial tracker
B) A fantasy sports or fitness tracking spreadsheet
C) A side project or small business model
D) I do not touch Excel on weekends and I am at peace with that

Drop what you are building if you are in the A, B, or C camp. Always interesting to see what this community does with Excel outside of work.

Your personal budget spreadsheet breaks every month because you are manually entering the month name and changing date ranges.

Fix it once and never touch it again.

Put this in your month header cell:
=TEXT(DATE(YEAR(TODAY()),MONTH(TODAY()),1),"MMMM YYYY")

Put this in your start date cell:
=DATE(YEAR(TODAY()),MONTH(TODAY()),1)

Put this in your end date cell:
=EOMONTH(TODAY(),0)

Now every formula that references those cells updates automatically on the first of each month. Your budget resets itself. Your date ranges update themselves. You open it on June 1st and it already says June.

Drop the personal spreadsheet you wish you had built this way from the start.

Real scenario:
"I track my expenses in Excel. I want to automatically categorize each transaction as Food, Transport, Rent, etc. based on the description column. Right now I do it manually."

Here is the formula approach:
=IFS(
ISNUMBER(SEARCH("Uber",A2)),"Transport",
ISNUMBER(SEARCH("Lyft",A2)),"Transport",
ISNUMBER(SEARCH("Whole Foods",A2)),"Food",
ISNUMBER(SEARCH("Trader Joe",A2)),"Food",
ISNUMBER(SEARCH("Rent",A2)),"Housing",
TRUE,"Uncategorized"
)

How it works: SEARCH looks for each keyword inside the description. ISNUMBER checks if it found something. IFS runs through your rules in order and returns the first match. The TRUE at the end is the catch-all for anything that does not match.

Add as many keyword rules as you need. The more specific your keywords the more accurate your categorization.

Drop your expense categorization nightmare below. If your descriptions are inconsistent I will show you how to handle that too.

New week starting. What is the one Excel skill you are committing to actually practicing this week?

A) Power Query — I keep saying I will learn it and I never do
B) Dynamic arrays — FILTER, SORT, UNIQUE, SEQUENCE
C) Building a proper dashboard from scratch
D) Getting faster with keyboard shortcuts until they are automatic

Drop yours and tell me what specifically you are going to do this week to make it real. Not just "practice it." The actual thing you are going to build or fix.

You are using keyboard shortcuts but you are still reaching for the mouse constantly because you have not learned the ones that matter most.

These five eliminate 80% of mouse usage for the average analyst:

Ctrl+Shift+L — toggle filters on and off
Ctrl+T — convert selection to Table instantly
Alt+= — AutoSum the selected range
Ctrl+Shift+End — select from current cell to last used cell
F4 — repeat last action (the most underused key in Excel)

The F4 one specifically. If you just formatted a cell, applied a border, inserted a row — press F4 and Excel does it again to whatever is selected next. Chained with Ctrl+clicking multiple cells this replaces dozens of repetitive formatting steps.

Drop the mouse habit you most want to break.
→ Full keyboard shortcut system in Excel Flow Edition #22: [Substack URL for Edition #22]

Real scenario:
"I have a list of 5,000 product names. Some are all caps, some are all lowercase, some are mixed. I need them all in proper title case."

One formula:
=PROPER(A2)

That is it. PROPER converts any text to Title Case where the first letter of each word is capitalized.

The catch: PROPER capitalizes the first letter after every space including things you might not want like "Xlookup" becoming "Xlookup" instead of "XLOOKUP." For those cases you need a follow-up SUBSTITUTE:
=SUBSTITUTE(PROPER(A2),"Xlookup","XLOOKUP")

For a full name cleaning pipeline that handles TRIM, PROPER, and common inconsistencies in one column:
=PROPER(TRIM(SUBSTITUTE(A2,"  "," ")))

This removes extra spaces, collapses double spaces, and proper-cases everything in a single formula.

Drop your worst data cleaning scenario below. If it involves text inconsistencies I will build you the formula live in the comments.
